coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs ternary core–shell heterostructures as a magnetically recoverable photocatalyst for selective alcohol oxidation coupled with H2O2 production

coredata.dc:description: Photocatalytic aerobic oxidation of aromatic alcohols to corresponding aldehydes coupled with producing hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) represents one of the most efficient strategies for converting solar energy into chemical energy. In this work, a magnetically recoverable photocatalyst of Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs ternary core–shell heterostructures is elaborately fabricated through the hydrothermal growth of CdS on Fe3O4 nanospheres with in-situ incorporation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and used for selective alcohol oxidation coupled with H2O2 production. The Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs photocatalyst possess distinct advantages of full solar spectral absorption, efficient charge separation, and high stability. The Fe3O4-nanosphere cores not only endow photocatalyst with the characteristics of magnetic recovery but also form Fe3O4@CdS Z-scheme heterojunction to prevent CdS from photocorrosion. The in-situ modified CQDs act as charge mediators to accelerate the photogenerated electron-hole separation and afford active sites to facilitate H2O2 production. As a result, the Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs photocatalyst exhibits excellent performance in selectively converting benzyl alcohol to benzaldehyde accompanied with H2O2 production. The generation rates of benzaldehyde and H2O2 reach up to 57.22 and 27.06 mmol·gCdS − 1·h− 1, respectively. This work highlights a rational construction of magnetic heterostructure photocatalyst and its application in the photo-redox coupling reactions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration for the synthesis of Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs core–shell heterostructures. (b) SEM, (c) TEM, (d) HRTEM and (e) STEM images and EDS elemental mapping of Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs.
(a) XRD pattern of Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs sample, (b) FT-IR spectra of CdS, CdS@CQDs and Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs samples.
XPS spectra of Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs sample. (a) Survey spectrum, (b) Fe 2p, (c) O 1 s, (d) Cd 3d, (e) S 2p and (f) C 1 s.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra and photographs (inset) of different photocatalysts, (b) Mott-Schottky plots measured at 1000 Hz of CdS and Fe3O4, (c) photocurrent response, (d) EIS Nyquist plots, (e) steady PL spectra (inset: amplified PL spectra of Fe3O4@CdS and Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs), and (f) time-resolved PL spectra of different photocatalysts.

(b) Photocatalytic performance of different catalysts and (c) Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs catalysts with different CQDs contents, (d) effects of conditions on the photocatalytic reaction, (e) transformations of different aromatic alcohols along with H2O2 production over Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs photocatalyst.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs ternary core–shell heterostructures as a magnetically recoverable photocatalyst for selective alcohol oxidation coupled with H2O2 production

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic aerobic oxidation of aromatic alcohols to corresponding aldehydes coupled with producing hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) represents one of the most efficient strategies for converting solar energy into chemical energy. In this work, a magnetically recoverable photocatalyst of Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs ternary core–shell heterostructures is elaborately fabricated through the hydrothermal growth of CdS on Fe3O4 nanospheres with in-situ incorporation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and used for selective alcohol oxidation coupled with H2O2 production. The Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs photocatalyst possess distinct advantages of full solar spectral absorption, efficient charge separation, and high stability. The Fe3O4-nanosphere cores not only endow photocatalyst with the characteristics of magnetic recovery but also form Fe3O4@CdS Z-scheme heterojunction to prevent CdS from photocorrosion. The in-situ modified CQDs act as charge mediators to accelerate the photogenerated electron-hole separation and afford active sites to facilitate H2O2 production. As a result, the Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs photocatalyst exhibits excellent performance in selectively converting benzyl alcohol to benzaldehyde accompanied with H2O2 production. The generation rates of benzaldehyde and H2O2 reach up to 57.22 and 27.06 mmol·gCdS − 1·h− 1, respectively. This work highlights a rational construction of magnetic heterostructure photocatalyst and its application in the photo-redox coupling reactions.

ce:section.ce:para: Selective oxidation of aromatic alcohols, as a kind of fundamental organic transformation, has gained a lot of attention in both laboratory and chemical industry, because the aromatic aldehydes are essential for the synthesis of fine chemicals and chemical intermediates [1,2]. The traditional methods for alcohol oxidation suffer from the usage of strong oxidizing reagents, such as permanganate and dichromate, which not only increases the production costs but also leads to a lot of heavy metal pollutants. Photocatalytic technology has provided an eco-friendly and sustainable chemical process with molecular oxygen (O2) as a low-cost and green oxidant for selective alcohol oxidation under solar irradiation [3–5], where O2 serves as a mild oxidant and easily combines with protons to produce H2O2 [6–9]. As a versatile chemical, H2O2 is widely used as a green and eco-friendly solar energy carrier in fuel cells, the multifunctional oxidizer for environmental treatment [10], and the essential chemical in diverse industries including chemical synthesis, paper and pulp, mining, and detergents [11–13]. The high demand is motivating researchers to develop sustainable routes for H2O2 production [14,15]. The photocatalytic synthesis of H2O2 has received growing interest to stand a chance of substituting the traditional ones with the anthraquinone oxidation processes [16–18]. The photocatalytic efficiency of H2O2 production is generally limited by the high oxidation potential of water required to supply protons. Using alcohols with low oxidation potentials as electron donors instead of water, to eliminate the photogenerated holes and afford protons, can significantly improve the production efficiency of H2O2 [8,19,20]. The photocatalytic redox coupling reaction of selective alcohol oxidation to aldehydes and O2 reduction to H2O2 is a more efficient strategy for solar energy conversion, which can get the utmost out of photoinduced electrons and holes to simultaneously produce valuable organic chemicals and H2O2 [21–23]. Therefore, the development of efficient and low-cost photocatalysts toward the simultaneous production of H2O2 and aromatic aldehydes are of great significance for promoting solar energy to chemical energy conversion.

The important factors in determining photocatalysis generally include the light-harvesting ability, separation efficiency of photoexcited electron-hole pairs as well as the catalytically active sites [24,25]. In addition, both the stability in long-term reaction and the convenience for recycling are also important to evaluate the practicability of photocatalysts. Single-component semiconductor photocatalysts usually suffer from narrow photo-response region and high recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Moreover, the powder catalyst is difficult to separate from the reaction solution, which increases the complication of post-treatment process. The photocorrosion, especially in CdS-based photocatalysts, significantly challenges their durable application [26,27]. To address these problems, constructing the magnetic core/shell semiconductor heterostructures can not only promote the spatial charge separation but also facilitate the recycling of photocatalyst with the help of an external magnetic field [28,29]. The core/shell nanostructures can afford a maximum heterointerface to accelerate the charge transfer between different semiconductors [30,31]. As a representative magnetic semiconductor, Fe3O4 is favorably used to fabricate magnetic heterojunction photocatalysts because of its strong magnetism, good stability and low cost [28,32,33]. Meanwhile, the intrinsic n-type semiconductor property of Fe3O4 is beneficial to improving the transfer efficiency of photoinduced charge carriers [28]. Therefore, the integration of Fe3O4 with a visible-light-responsive semiconductor, such as CdS, into a core–shell nanostructure is a promising strategy for exploiting the magnetically recoverable photocatalyst with high performance.

Additionally, the introduction of solid-state electron mediators at the interface between semiconductors can further accelerate the separation and transfer of photoinduced charge carriers in the heterojunction photocatalysts [28,34,35]. As an inexpensive metal-free alternative, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted increasing interests to serve as electron mediators in heterojunction photocatalysts owing to the excellent photogenerated electron trapping ability and favorable charge transfer efficiency [28,34,36–38]. Moreover, CQDs can act as a cocatalyst to afford the active sites for photocatalytic H2O2 production via two-electron oxygen reduction [39,40]. Therefore, the incorporation of CQDs into semiconductor heterostructures is promising to further enhance the performance of photocatalyst. The general methods of adding the prefabricated CQDs into synthetic systems may result in uneven distribution and unstable anchoring of CQDs in photocatalysts [41,42], while the synthesis of semiconductor heterostructures with in-situ incorporation of CQDs is a more favorable choice which not only simplify the synthesis steps but also strengthen the combination between CQDs and semiconductors. Specifically, the advantages and disadvantages of different synthetic methods for fabricating the composite photocatalysts consisting of CQDs and CdS or Fe3O4 are summarized in Table S1 (Supporting Information).

Based on the above considerations, herein, the Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs ternary core–shell heterostructures are synthesized via a hydrothermal process where CdS nanoparticles (NPs) are coated on Fe3O4 nanospheres and CQDs are in-situ generated to uniformly distribute in CdS shell. In the synthesis, ascorbic acid (AA) is added to act as a complexing agent to control the sizes of CdS NPs as well as serve as a carbon source being in-situ converted into CQDs during the hydrothermal process. The Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs heterostructures are used as a magnetically recoverable photocatalyst for selective alcohol oxidation coupled with H2O2 production. Benefiting from the Z-scheme heterojunction formed at the interface of Fe3O4 and CdS with CQDs as both electron mediator and cocatalyst, the Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs photocatalyst exhibits excellent performance for the synchronous production of benzaldehyde and H2O2, together with the high magnetic recoverability and long-term stability. The role of each component in the ternary core–shell photocatalyst is studied systematically, and the possible photocatalytic mechanism is proposed.

Iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O, AR), sodium acetate anhydrous (CH3COONa, AR), cadmium acetate dihydrate (C4H6CdO4·2H2O, AR), thiourea (CH4N2S, 99%), potassium phthalate monobasic (HOOCC6H4COOK, AR), potassium iodide (KI, AR), acetonitrile (CH3CN, chromatographic grade), sodium sulfate anhydrous (Na2SO4, 99%) and benzyl alcohol (C7H8O, 99%) were purchased from Aladdin biochemical technology Co., Ltd. Trisodium citrate dihydrate (C6H5Na3O7·2H2O, AR) and l-ascorbic acid (C6H8O6, AR) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ethylene glycol (C2H6O2, AR) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. The chemicals were used without further purification.

The Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs core–shell heterostructures are synthesized by a two-step process. Firstly, Fe3O4 nanospheres are synthesized by a reported recipe with minor modifications [43], and the details are shown in Supporting Information. Subsequently, the Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs core–shell heterostructures are synthesized via a hydrothermal method. Specifically, 60 mg of the as-prepared Fe3O4 nanospheres is dispersed in 30 mL deionized water by ultrasound, followed with the successive addition of ascorbic acid (18.3 mg), cadmium acetate (55.3 mg) and thiourea (23.7 mg) under continuous stirring. The final suspension is transferred into a hydrothermal autoclave with 50 mL capacity, which is kept at 180 °C for 5 h. After the hydrothermal process, the solid particles are collected by centrifugation, washed with deionized water and ethanol for several times, and dried at 60 °C in a vacuum oven, to obtain the Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs heterostructures. By varying the dosage of ascorbic acid for the synthesis, a series of samples with different CQDs contents are synthesized and denoted as Fe3O4@CdS@0.3CQDs (10.9 mg AA), Fe3O4@CdS@0.5CQDs (18.3 mg AA), and Fe3O4@CdS@0.7CQDs (25.6 mg AA), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CdS, CdS@CQDs and Fe3O4@CdS samples
ce:section.ce:para: Several control samples, such as CdS, CdS@CQDs and Fe3O4@CdS, are synthesized by the similar procedure to that of preparing Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs photocatalyst, with the absence of Fe3O4 and/or ascorbic acid, respectively.

The morphology, microstructure and elemental distribution of photocatalysts are characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Zeiss, sigma500) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI tecnai G2 F20) equipped with an energy disperse spectrometer (EDS). The components of photocatalysts are analyzed by X-ray diffractometer (XRD, DX-2700) and Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Thermo Fisher Scientific 5225 Veronal Rd Madison, WI. 53711). The CdS contents in CdS@CQDs, Fe3O4@CdS and Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs samples are determined by atomic absorption spectrometer (Shimadzu, AA-7000). The specific surface areas are measured by Brunauer-Emmet-Teller (BET) method through N2 adsorption/desorption process on a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 v4.01 analyzer at 77 K, and the pore size distributions are obtained by Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) are recorded by X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS, DX-2700 X-ray diffractometer) and the binding energies are calibrated by C 1 s peak at 284.8 eV. The magnetic hysteresis curve is acquired by a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM, Quantum Design PPMS DynaCool, LakeShore7404, MPMS XL-7, PPMS-9). The optical absorption properties of different photocatalysts are evaluated by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS, Shimadzu, UV-2600 spectrophotometer). Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra and time-resolved fluorescence decay dynamics of different photocatalysts are recorded through a steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FS5 fluorescence spectrometer), with an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The intermediate radicals generated in photocatalytic system are analyzed by electron spin resonance (ESR) spectrometer (Bruker JEOL JES-FA200) using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as a spin-trapping reagent.

Photoelectrochemical tests are performed on a CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua). Photocurrent response, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and Mott-Schottky (M−S) plot are measured by a standard three-electrode system: working electrode prepared by coating 3 mg photocatalyst on a fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) conductive glass (1 × 2 cm), Ag/AgCl reference electrode, and Pt wire counter electrode. The electrolyte solution is 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution. A 300 W Xenon lamp (CEL-HXF300, Beijing China Education Au Light) is used to provide the irradiation with wavelengths in 300–780 nm region and an optical power density of 300 mW·cm−2. All the photoelectrochemical tests are carried out at room temperature.

The performance of different photocatalysts toward selective alcohol oxidation coupled with H2O2 production is evaluated by the photocatalytic reaction in a quartz tube with length of 15 cm and inner diameter of 1.3 cm. For a typical photocatalytic reaction, the suspension containing 5 mg photocatalyst and 2 mL benzyl alcohol is transferred into the quartz tube, which is bubbled with O2 gas for 30 min and then sealed by a balloon full of O2 gas. The reaction suspension is exposed to a simulated sunlight (300 nm < λ < 780 nm, 300 mW·cm−2) provided by the Xenon lamp (CEL-HXF300, Beijing China Education Au Light). After 4 h irradiation, the catalyst powder is removed from the reaction system by an external magnetic field and the filtration through a 0.22 µm polymer membrane. The reaction solution is diluted to 10 mL by acetonitrile and then quantificationally analyzed by a high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Shimadzu, LC-2030 Plus) with a mobile phase containing 25% acetonitrile and 75% phosphate buffer solution (phosphate concentration: 1 vol‰) at a flow rate of 0.9 mL/min. The amount of H2O2 generated in the photocatalytic reaction is determined by the iodometric method [9]. The mixture of 1 mL potassium hydrogen phthalate aqueous solution (0.1 M), 1 mL potassium iodide aqueous solution (0.4 M) and 1 mL of the acetonitrile-diluted reacted solution is kept at ambient conditions for 10 min to achieve the complete reaction (H2O2 + 3I− + 2H+ → I3 − + 2H2O). The amount of I3 − is determined by UV–visible spectroscopy according to its characteristic absorption at 350 nm, and the corresponding amount of H2O2 is calculated. Additionally, the cycling stabilities of different photocatalysts are evaluated by four cycles of photocatalytic reactions. After each cycle, the catalyst is separated from the solution and then dispersed into new benzyl alcohol for the next reaction cycle.

The Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs ternary core–shell heterojunction photocatalyst is elaborately synthesized through a two-step process, where Fe3O4 nanospheres are firstly synthesized via a solvothermal process and subsequently coated by CdS NPs along with in-situ incorporation of CQDs via a hydrothermal process (Fig. 1 a). In the synthesis, ascorbic acid (AA) acts as a complexing agent to control the uniform growth of CdS NPs around Fe3O4 nanospheres and as a carbon source to be in-situ converted into CQDs. The Fe3O4 nanospheres (Fig. S1a, Supporting Information) synthesized by the first solvothermal step present relatively smooth surface and have diameters of 120–220 nm. After coating CdS NPs and CQDs, the as-prepared Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs core–shell heterostructures have larger diameters of 160–230 nm and rough surfaces (Fig. 1b). TEM (Fig. 1c) and HRTEM (Fig. 1d) images of Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs sample further reveal that the shell consists of numerous small nanoparticles with sizes of 5–10 nm. Moreover, the HRTEM image (Fig. 1d) shows three different lattice spacings, corresponding to (111) lattice plane of Fe3O4, (100) lattice plane of CdS, and (002) lattice plane of CQDs [44,45], respectively. In contrast, both pure CdS (Fig. S1b, Supporting Information) and Fe3O4@CdS (Fig. S1c, Supporting Information) synthesized in the absence of ascorbic acid are composed of larger nanoparticles, suggesting that ascorbic acid plays an important role in controlling the sizes of CdS NPs. This is also supported by the comparison between the particle sizes of pure CdS and CdS@CQDs (Fig. S1d, Supporting Information). The EDS elemental mappings indicate that both CdS and CQDs are uniformly distributed around Fe3O4 nanospheres, as shown in Fig. 1e and Fig. S2 (Supporting Information). Based on the above characterizations including SEM, TEM and HRTEM images, it can be concluded that the Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs ternary core–shell heterostructures have been synthesized. Since ascorbic acid can act as a complexing agent to help the deposition of CdS NPs around Fe3O4 nanospheres [46,47] as well as a carbon source being in-situ converted into CQDs [48,49], it is inferred that the CQDs evenly distribute throughout the CdS shells and also exist at the interface between Fe3O4 and CdS.

From XRD characterizations (Fig. S3, Supporting Information), the pure CdS is attributable to a hexagonal crystalline structure (JCPDS No. 75–1545), and the Fe3O4 nanospheres have the cubic crystalline structure (JCPDS No. 74–0748). XRD pattern of Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs ternary photocatalyst (Fig. 2 a) contains the diffraction peaks of both Fe3O4 and CdS. CdS@CQDs and Fe3O4@CdS binary photocatalysts show the similar XRD patterns with those of pure CdS and Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs ternary photocatalyst, respectively. The diffraction peaks of CQDs cannot be detected in CdS@CQDs and Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs samples, due to the low content and weak crystallinity of CQDs [50]. The actual CdS contents of CdS@CQDs, Fe3O4@CdS and Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs are measured to be 90.13, 29.53 and 26.67 wt% (Table S2, Supporting Information), respectively, by atomic absorption spectrometry. Fig. 2b displays the FT-IR spectra of CdS, CdS@CQDs and Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs samples. Both CdS@CQDs and Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs exhibit the characteristic IR signals corresponding to the functional groups of CQDs, where the peaks at 1605, 1404 and 1300 cm−1 are assignable to the stretching vibrations of CO, CH and COH bonds, respectively [51,52]. In contrast, pure CdS shows the extremely weak IR signals in the range of 1000 ∼ 4000 cm−1, suggesting that the influence of water in samples can be excluded. These results further confirm that CQDs have been in-situ incorporated in the core–shell heterostructures via the carbonization of ascorbic acid during hydrothermal process. The specific surface areas and pore features of CdS, Fe3O4@CdS, and Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs samples are determined by N2 adsorption–desorption measurements (Fig. S4 and Table S3, Supporting Information). Pure CdS has a very small specific surface area of 4.068 m2·g−1 and negligible pore volume (0.003 cm3·g−1) because of its larger particle sizes and severe agglomeration. Both Fe3O4@CdS and Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs exhibit the much larger specific surface areas and pore volumes than those of pure CdS, due to the reduced particle sizes of CdS and the uniform dispersion of core–shell nanostructures. In comparison, the Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs ternary core–shell nanostructures shows the largest surface area and pore volume, which is undoubtedly beneficial to improving the photocatalytic performance.

The surface elements and their valence states of Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs photocatalyst are analyzed by XPS characterization (Fig. 3 ). The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 3a) shows obvious signals of Fe, O, Cd, S and C elements. The Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 3b) can be deconvoluted into six peaks, where the peaks at 710.4 and 724.1 eV correspond to Fe2+ ions, while the peaks at 712.7 and 726.7 eV are assigned to Fe3+ ions, and the others belong to Fe 2p satellite peaks [53]. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3c) is deconvoluted into three peaks with binding energies of 529.6, 531.1 and 532.6 eV, respectively. The peak at 529.6 eV is ascribed to the lattice oxygen of Fe3O4, while the peaks at 531.1 and 532.6 eV are assigned to the CO/CO groups (531.1 eV) of CQDs and the surface-absorbed H2O (532.6 eV) [34], respectively. The characteristic peaks in Cd 3d and S 2p spectra (Fig. 3d and 3e) are well ascribed to Cd2+ and S2- of CdS [54], respectively. In C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3f), the peaks at 284.8, 285.9 and 288.8 eV are attributable to the C species related to CC, CO and CO bonds of CQDs [51], respectively, confirming that CQDs are in-situ incorporated into the core–shell heterostructures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoabsorption properties and photogenerated charge carriers
ce:section.ce:para: The photoabsorption properties of different photocatalysts (CdS, CdS@CQDs, Fe3O4, Fe3O4@CdS and Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs) are determined by UV–vis DRS spectroscopy, and the Kubelka-Munk transformed absorption spectra are shown in Fig. 4 a. Pure CdS shows a strong photo-absorption in 200–550 nm region, corresponding to its bandgap (Eg) of 2.25 eV (Fig. S5a, Supporting Information). The incorporation of CQDs makes the CdS@CQDs photocatalyst have obvious photoabsorption in λ > 550 nm region, and the CdS@CQDs sample shows a deeper color (brown) than that of pure CdS (yellow). The black Fe3O4 nanospheres can absorb the light of entire UV–visible region, related to its bandgap (Eg) of 1.91 eV (Fig. S5b, Supporting Information). The Fe3O4@CdS sample also shows obvious photoabsorption in λ > 550 nm region and present the dark-brown color. Accordingly, the Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs ternary photocatalyst exhibits the full-spectrum absorption to UV–visible light as well as a deeper color than that of Fe3O4@CdS sample due to the incorporation of CQDs. To determine the electronic band structures of photocatalysts, the Mott-Schottky (M−S) plots of catalyst-electrodes are recorded to acquire the flat-band potentials of CdS and Fe3O4 (Fig. 4b), respectively. The reliability of the results is confirmed by the M−S plots measured at different frequencies (Figs. S5c-d, Supporting Information). The linear regions of the M−S plots have positive slopes, indicating that both CdS and Fe3O4 are n-type semiconductors. The flat-band potentials of CdS and Fe3O4 are deduced to be −1.27 and −0.28 V (vs. Ag/AgCl, pH = 7), respectively, which can be translated into the corresponding values of −1.07 and −0.08 V (vs. NHE) [55]. Since the conduction band minimum (CBM) of n-type semiconductor is close to its flat band potential, the valence band maxima (VBM) of CdS and Fe3O4 can be estimated to be 1.18 and 1.83 V according to the relationship of Eg = EVBM - ECBM [51]. The photocurrent measurements are used to evaluate the separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes in different photocatalysts, as shown in Fig. 4c. Pure Fe3O4 nanospheres barely generate photocurrent under the irradiation due to the fast recombination of photogenerated charge carriers. Both Fe3O4@CdS and CdS@CQDs show a little increase in the photocurrent intensities, compared with Fe3O4 and CdS. In contrast, the Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs ternary heterojunction photocatalyst exhibits the highest photocurrent intensity, confirming that the simultaneous incorporations of Fe3O4 and CQDs can synergistically enhance the separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is used to examine the charge transfer ability of the photocatalysts (Fig. 4d). In comparison, the Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs photoelectrode shows the smallest arc radius in EIS Nyquist plots, indicating that the in-situ incorporation of CQDs can greatly accelerate the interfacial charge transfer in the heterojunction photocatalyst. Furthermore, the steady state photoluminescence (PL) spectra of different photocatalysts are measured to further evaluate the photoexcited electron-hole separation efficiencies (Fig. 4e). Under the 380 nm excitation, pure CdS exhibits a strong PL emission band around 575 nm, corresponding to the radiative recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes in CdS [56]. In comparison, the PL intensity of CdS@CQDs sample decreases obviously, indicating that the introduction of CQDs is beneficial to the separation of photogenerated charge [57]. Particularly, the PL emissions of both Fe3O4@CdS and Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs heterojunction photocatalysts are almost completely quenched, where the intensity of Fe3O4@CdS is a little higher than that of Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs, suggesting that the recombination of photoexcited electrons and holes has been suppressed in the materials. The time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra (Fig. 4f) are recorded to determine the photoexcited charge carrier dynamics of different photocatalysts. All the TRPL decay curves can be fitted by the biexponential function and the fluorescence lifetimes of different photocatalysts are listed in Table S10 (Supporting Information). In comparison, the Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs ternary core–shell photocatalyst presents the shortest fluorescence lifetime, corresponding to the most efficient separation of photoexcited electrons and holes in this photocatalyst [58].

The performance of various photocatalysts toward the selective alcohol oxidation coupled with H2O2 production (Fig. 5 a) is examined. The production rates of benzaldehyde (BAD) and H2O2 in the presence of different photocatalysts are shown in Fig. 5b and Table S4 (Supporting Information). The generated amount of H2O2 in the photocatalytic reaction is measured by iodometric method (Fig. S6, Supporting Information). Since the Fe3O4 nanospheres have negligible photocatalysis for the productions of benzaldehyde and H2O2, the photocatalytic production rates are normalized by the CdS weight in each CdS-contained photocatalyst, according to the actual CdS content measured by atomic absorption spectrometry (Table S2, Supporting Information). In comparison, the production rates of both benzaldehyde and H2O2 over CdS@CQDs photocatalyst are higher than two-fold those over pure CdS, which can be ascribed to the reduced particle sizes of CdS in CdS@CQDs [59,60] as well as the promoting effect of CQDs on photocatalysis [41,50,61]. The Fe3O4@CdS photocatalyst has the comparable activity for benzaldehyde production with that of CdS@CQDs but shows the inferior H2O2-production efficiency. Compared with Fe3O4@CdS sample, the Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs ternary core–shell photocatalysts exhibit the enhanced production rates of both benzaldehyde and H2O2. The decrease in H2O2 generation of Fe3O4@CdS and Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs core–shell photocatalysts, compared with CdS@CQDs sample, is possibly caused by the Fe3O4-accelerated decomposition of H2O2 in reaction solution, where the Fe3O4-nanosphere cores act as a Fe-based Fenton-like catalyst and thus accelerate the conversion of H2O2 into hydroxyl radicals to further oxidize benzyl alcohol into benzaldehyde [62]. The control experiments (Fig. S7, Supporting Information) testify that the addition of a little H2O2 (230 μmol) in the photocatalytic system can promote the production rate of benzaldehyde. Furthermore, the CQDs content of Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs ternary core/shell photocatalyst is optimized for the photocatalytic reaction, as shown in Fig. 5c. A proper content of CQDs in the photocatalyst is important to afford sufficient active sites while not to block the light absorption of CdS [63]. In consequence, the production rates of benzaldehyde and H2O2 can reach up to 57.22 and 27.06 mmol·gCdS − 1·h− 1, respectively, over Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs photocatalyst.

Subsequently, a series of control experiments are conducted to probe the influence of several conditions on the photocatalytic reaction. As shown in Fig. 5d and Table S5 (Supporting Information), both photocatalyst and light irradiation are indispensable for the selective oxidation of benzyl alcohol along with H2O2 production in the present reaction system. Besides, molecular oxygen also plays an important role in the photocatalytic redox coupling reaction. Under N2 atmosphere, only a small amount of benzaldehyde is produced but no H2O2 is generated. Compared to the photocatalytic reaction conducted in air, the higher production rates of benzaldehyde and H2O2 are obtained in pure O2 atmosphere. These results confirm that the presence of O2 is requisite for the generation of H2O2 and also greatly promotes the transformation of benzyl alcohol to benzaldehyde. Therefore, the Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs ternary core–shell photocatalyst shows a favorable performance toward the simultaneous conversion of benzyl alcohol and O2 to benzaldehyde and H2O2. Furthermore, the Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs photocatalyst is used toward the selective oxidation of benzyl-alcohol derivatives with different substituent groups together with H2O2 production under the same conditions (300 mW·cm−2, UV–vis irradiation, 2 mL alcohol), as shown in Fig. 5e and Table S6 (Supporting Information). The results indicate that the Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs photocatalyst has a good universality in the photocatalytic selective oxidation of aromatic alcohols coupled with H2O2 production. The higher production rates of benzaldehyde (59.34 mmol·gCdS − 1·h− 1) and H2O2 (35.32 mmol·gCdS − 1·h− 1) are achieved in the photocatalytic reaction system of p-methoxybenzyl alcohol, possibly because the electron-donating group on benzene ring facilitate the h+-driven oxidation of –CH2OH [64].

The stabilities of several different photocatalysts are examined by the photocatalytic cycle testing (4 h irradiation for each cycle). As shown in Fig. 6 a and 6c, Table S7 and S9 (Supporting Information), the photocatalytic activity of Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs is well maintained after 4 cycles, while pure CdS presents a great decrease in photocatalysis, indicating that the construction of Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs ternary core–shell heterostructures can prevent CdS from photocorrosion and greatly improves the stability of photocatalyst. In addition, as shown in Fig. 6b and Table S8, the Fe3O4@CdS binary core–shell photocatalyst also shows a good stability in cycling test, suggesting that Fe3O4 cores can capture the photogenerated holes and therefore prevent CdS from photocorrosion. Then, we further measured the XRD patterns and XPS spectra of pure CdS and Fe3O4@CdS samples before and after photocatalytic cycling test, respectively, to probe the effect of Fe3O4 cores on the stability of CdS shells. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the XRD peaks of pure CdS are weakened after photocatalytic reaction, suggesting that the crystallinity of CdS decreases because of the photocorrosion during photocatalytic process. Whereas, there is no obvious change in the XRD peaks of Fe3O4@CdS core–shell structures (Fig. 7b) after photocatalytic reaction, suggesting that Fe3O4 cores can suppress the photocorrosion of CdS. Furthermore, the peak of S6+ species (at ∼ 168.9 eV) appears obviously in the S 2p XPS spectrum of pure CdS (Fig. 7c) after photocatalytic reaction cycles, which is attributable to the oxidation of CdS into SO4 2- by the photogenerated holes and reactive oxygen species [65,66]. In contrast, there is no variation in the S 2p spectrum of Fe3O4@CdS sample (Fig. 7d) after photocatalytic reaction, confirming that Fe3O4 cores can effectively prevent CdS from photocorrosion. The magnetic Fe3O4 cores also enable the Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs photocatalyst easily recyclable from the reaction solution with using an external magnet, which is economic and green in comparison to the traditional separation methods. The magnetic property of the Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs photocatalyst is measured by vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) analysis at room temperature. The saturation magnetization (Ms) of Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs photocatalyst is measured to be 48.7 emu·g−1 (Fig. 6d), and this photocatalyst can be readily separated from the reaction solution by a common magnet (inset in Fig. 6d).

To further explore the photocatalytic mechanism of Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs ternary core/shell heterostructures, in-situ ESR detection is employed to reveal the generation of radical intermediates during the photocatalytic reaction. As shown in Fig. 8 a, no ESR signal is observed from the reaction system in dark. Upon 10 min irradiation of the simulated sunlight, ESR signals of both carbon-centered radical and superoxide radical (·O2 –) are observed obviously, suggesting that the photocatalytic production of H2O2 is achieved by a multi-step one-electron process [18,67]. The reduction ability of photogenerated electrons in the conduction band of Fe3O4 (ECB = -0.08 V) is not enough to convert O2 to superoxide radical (-0.33 V vs. NHE) [18,68]. Moreover, the photocatalytic stabilities of both Fe3O4@CdS and Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs core/shell heterostructures are much higher than that of CdS, suggesting that Fe3O4 cores can capture the photogenerated holes from CdS and prevent CdS from photocorrosion. These results indicate that the separation and migration of photogenerated electrons and holes in the Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs core/shell heterostructures follow the Z-scheme semiconductor-heterojunction mechanism instead of Type-II one, as shown in Fig. 8b. Under the irradiation, both Fe3O4 and CdS are excited to generate electron-hole pairs. The photogenerated electrons of Fe3O4 cores readily migrate to CdS shells and recombine with the photogenerated holes, where CQDs at the interface serve as electron bridges to facilitate the charge transfer. In consequence, the photogenerated electrons and holes are well separated in the conduction band of CdS shells and the valence band of Fe3O4 cores, respectively, which not only prolongs the photogenerated charge lifetime but also prevents CdS from photocorrosion. The photoinduced electrons accumulated on CdS shells are captured by the surface-anchored CQDs which afford the active sites for the O2 reduction into ·O2 – radical. Meanwhile, the photogenerated holes (h+) on Fe3O4 are consumed by the adsorbed benzyl-alcohol molecules to generate the active intermediate of carbon-centered radical. Finally, both H2O2 and benzaldehyde are produced by the proton transfer from organic intermediate to ·O2 – radical. Therefore, the Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst of Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs ternary core–shell nanostructures possess distinct advantages including broad light-harvesting range, efficient charge separation, and sufficient active sites, which jointly contribute to the enhanced photocatalysis. The Fe3O4 nanosphere cores endow the photocatalyst with a magnetically recoverable function as well as improve the photo-stability of photocatalyst by regulating the charge transfer. The in-situ incorporated CQDs act as electron mediators to facilitate the charge transfer between Fe3O4 and CdS and also a cocatalyst to accelerate the reduction of O2 to H2O2.

In summary, the Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs ternary core–shell heterostructures are designedly synthesized via a two-step procedure and used as a magnetically recoverable photocatalyst for the coproduction of benzaldehyde and H2O2 from aerobic oxidation of benzyl alcohol under a simulated sunlight. For the photocatalyst synthesis, ascorbic acid is employed to serve as a complexing agent to control the uniform deposition of small CdS NPs as well as a carbon source to achieve the in-situ incorporation of CQDs into CdS shell. The Fe3O4-nanosphere cores not only endow the photocatalyst with magnetically recoverable function but also form Fe3O4@CdS Z-scheme heterojunction to prevent CdS from photocorrosion, and the in-situ incorporated CQDs act as electron mediators to facilitate the photogenerated electron-hole separation as well as afford the active sites to accelerate the O2 reduction. Therefore, the Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs heterojunction photocatalyst exhibits the distinct advantages of full solar spectral absorption, efficient charge separation, and high photo-stability. For the photocatalytic reaction, Fe3O4@CdS@CQDs photocatalyst shows the high production rates of benzaldehyde and H2O2 up to 57.22 and 27.06 mmol·gCdS − 1·h− 1, respectively, and also exhibits the general applicability and excellent long-term stability. The photocatalytic mechanism has been verified by a series of control experiments and characterizations. This study demonstrates the rational design and synthesis of a multifunctional heterojunction photocatalyst and its application in the synchronous production of aromatic aldehydes and H2O2 under simulated sunlight.
